content:
Ryan, a couple of thoughts offered gently and plainly.

- God is honored not by us picking the view that wins the fewest eye-rolls, but by humble submission to what He actually said (Isaiah 66:2; Galatians 1:10). At the same time, we’re not called to add needless offense. The gospel is already “a stumbling block” (1 Corinthians 1:23); we shouldn’t create extra ones.

- “Literal” in the historic Christian sense means “according to the author’s intent,” not “woodenly literalistic.” Scripture often speaks in everyday, observational terms—sunrise, the ends of the earth, windows of heaven—without intending a physics lesson. Inerrancy has always allowed for that. The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy even notes the Bible’s use of phenomenological language.

- On the firmament (raqia): faithful, inerrantist readers disagree. Some see a solid vault; many see an “expanse/sky,” the realm that separates waters and in which lights are placed, described in ordinary, temple-tinged, ancient language. Others emphasize Genesis 1’s functional focus—God ordering and assigning roles—rather than material composition. None of these require denying that Scripture is true in all it affirms.

- So is God “big enough to be honored either way”? In this sense, yes: He is honored when His people, in good conscience, receive His Word reverently and interpret it responsibly, even if they land in different places on a secondary matter. What we must not do is reshape our reading merely to lower social cost. Nor should we bind consciences where Scripture hasn’t.

- Does insisting on a particular literalistic reading make Christianity unnecessarily hard? Sometimes it can, if we elevate a debated interpretation to a test of fidelity. Keep the scandal where Scripture puts it—on the cross and the resurrection—and hold non-essentials with humility (Romans 14; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

- With your kids, model both courage and carefulness: love the Bible, admit what’s plain and what’s debated, and show how Christians can be intellectually honest without being embarrassed by God’s Word. Teach them to answer simply: “The Bible’s purpose here is to show God’s ordering and goodness; its sky-language is ordinary, not a science claim.”

If you want to work toward a settled conscience on this, I’d suggest reading Genesis with an eye to genre and purpose, and consulting a few careful voices who affirm inerrancy but differ on raqia (for example, Calvin’s comments on God accommodating His speech to our capacity; C. John Collins; Vern Poythress). I’m glad to walk through specific passages with you.
